grant_request_details.project_title: White Pine Community Pollinator Garden and Outdoor Classroom

project_leader__organization.entity_if_applicable: Friends of White Pine Neighborhood Association
project_leader__organization.entity_address: Friends of White Pine Neighborhood Association, 214 Cedar Ridge Lane, White Pine, MT 59845

project_location__description.physical_address_of_project_location: White Pine Community Park, 102 Pine Meadow Drive, White Pine, MT 59845
project_location__description.project_description:
Overview:
This project will establish a permanent pollinator garden and small outdoor classroom space at White Pine Community Park in White Pine, Montana. The goal is to convert an underused, weedy 900-square-foot patch of grass near the existing playground into a native-plant demonstration garden with seating, educational signage, and a simple rainwater catchment system. The space will serve as a hands-on learning site for local families, the nearby White Pine Elementary School, and community groups interested in native plants, pollinator health, and low-water landscaping.

Objectives:
1. Create a healthy pollinator habitat using primarily native plant species that support bees, butterflies, and other beneficial insects.
2. Provide an outdoor learning area where local teachers, homeschool families, and youth groups can hold small classes, science activities, and nature-based lessons.
3. Reduce water use and maintenance needs in a currently turf-heavy section of the park by replacing mowed lawn with drought-tolerant plantings and mulch.
4. Engage neighborhood residents in at least three hands-on volunteer work days focused on planting, mulching, and ongoing maintenance.
5. Install clear, durable educational signage that explains the benefits of native plants, pollinators, and water-wise landscaping in language accessible to children and adults.

Background and Need:
White Pine is a small rural community, and our main park is heavily used for sports and playground activities but has very little in the way of naturalized or educational plantings. The section we plan to transform is currently a compacted lawn area that the Parks Department has identified as difficult to irrigate evenly. In summer 2024, several parents, including myself, noticed that the area became patchy and dusty, and kids tended to avoid it because it was either muddy or bare.

At the same time, our local elementary school has been trying to add more place-based science learning but has limited on-campus space for garden beds. I work part-time as an environmental educator and have been informally leading nature walks for neighborhood kids. When I proposed a small pollinator garden to the Friends of White Pine Neighborhood Association, the idea quickly grew into a more structured outdoor classroom concept, especially after two local teachers and a 4-H leader expressed interest in having a dedicated site where they could reliably bring students.

Project Components:
1. Site Preparation (early spring):
   - Coordinate with the White Pine Parks Department to mark irrigation lines and confirm exact boundaries of the garden area (approximately 30 ft x 30 ft, just east of the playground and south of the walking path).
   - Organize a volunteer work day to remove existing turf using a combination of hand tools and a rented sod cutter (the sod will be repurposed to patch bare spots elsewhere in the park).
   - Loosen compacted soil with shovels and broadforks, and incorporate a 2–3 inch layer of locally sourced compost to improve soil structure and water retention.

2. Planting Design and Materials:
   - The garden will focus on native and regionally adapted perennials and shrubs that bloom from early spring through late fall to provide continuous forage for pollinators.
   - Planned species include: blanketflower (Gaillardia aristata), purple coneflower (Echinacea purpurea), Rocky Mountain bee plant (Cleome serrulata), milkweed (Asclepias speciosa) for monarchs, yarrow (Achillea millefolium), penstemon, lupine, and several low-growing groundcovers to reduce weeds.
   - We will also incorporate a few small, hardy shrubs such as serviceberry and rabbitbrush to provide structure and winter interest.
   - Plants will be arranged in drifts and clusters rather than in straight rows, both for aesthetic reasons and to make it easier for pollinators to forage.

3. Mulching and Water Management:
   - After planting, we will apply 3–4 inches of wood chip mulch, sourced from a local tree service, to suppress weeds and conserve moisture.
   - We plan to install a simple, above-ground, 50–75 gallon rain barrel that collects water from the roof of the adjacent storage shed (already approved by the Parks Department). This will be used for supplemental hand-watering during the first two growing seasons while plants establish.
   - Existing drip irrigation lines in the area will be checked and, if functional, used to provide occasional deep watering during prolonged dry spells.

4. Outdoor Classroom Features:
   - We will create a small semi-circle of three sturdy wooden benches (each seating 3–4 children) facing the main section of the garden. These benches will be built from weather-resistant lumber and anchored to the ground for safety.
   - The seating area will be large enough to accommodate a small class (10–12 students) or a family group, but compact enough to blend naturally into the park setting.
   - A simple, removable chalkboard or whiteboard (weather-protected) will be mounted on a nearby fence post or on a dedicated post, allowing educators to write plant names, draw diagrams, or outline activities during lessons.

5. Educational Signage:
   - We will design and install two weather-resistant interpretive signs:
     a) "Welcome to the White Pine Pollinator Garden" – explaining what pollinators are, why they’re important, and how this garden helps them.
     b) "Water-Wise Gardening in Our Community" – describing how mulching, native plants, and rainwater capture can reduce water use.
   - Signs will include illustrations, plant ID photos, and simple, bilingual text (English and Spanish) because several families in the neighborhood speak Spanish at home.
   - A small QR code on each sign will link to a simple website (maintained by the Friends of White Pine) with plant lists, seasonal updates, and suggestions for similar plantings at home.

6. Community Engagement and Education:
   - We will host at least three volunteer work days: one for site prep and turf removal, one for planting, and one for mulching and sign installation. Families will be encouraged to "adopt" a section of the garden for seasonal weeding and watering.
   - I have already spoken with two teachers at White Pine Elementary (Ms. Lopez, 3rd grade, and Mr. Jensen, 5th grade), who are interested in using the space for science units on life cycles, ecosystems, and weather. We plan to coordinate at least four class visits during the first growing season.
   - I also lead a small weekend "Nature Club" for neighborhood kids (ages 6–11). We will use the garden to teach basic plant identification, insect observation, and simple data collection (for example, counting pollinator visits over a set time period and graphing the results).

Timeline:
- February–March 2026: Finalize plant list and layout, order plants and materials, confirm rain barrel setup with Parks Department, and design signage (including translation and graphics).
- April 2026: First volunteer work day for turf removal, soil preparation, and compost incorporation. Mark paths and bed edges.
- May 2026: Main planting weekend with volunteers. Install initial mulch layer and check irrigation and rain barrel system.
- June–July 2026: Install benches and classroom seating area. Host first official outdoor lesson with White Pine Elementary. Begin regular maintenance (weeding, watering as needed).
- August–September 2026: Install permanent educational signage. Host a community "Garden Open House" with a brief talk about pollinators and a guided plant walk.
- Ongoing (beyond grant period): Seasonal maintenance, plant replacement as needed, and continued use by school and community groups.

Budget and Use of Funds (requested $875):
- Native plants and shrubs (approximately 80–100 plugs and small pots): $450
- Compost and soil amendments: $100
- Mulch (delivery fee for wood chips, if needed): $75
- Lumber and hardware for three benches: $150
- Materials and printing for two outdoor-grade educational signs: $100

Any additional costs, such as rain barrel hardware, volunteer refreshments, and small tools, will be covered by in-kind donations from the Friends of White Pine Neighborhood Association and community members. I have already secured a commitment from a local hardware store for a discount on lumber and fasteners and from a tree service company willing to drop off a load of chipped wood at no cost when they are working nearby.

Long-Term Maintenance and Sustainability:
The Friends of White Pine Neighborhood Association has agreed to add the pollinator garden to its list of ongoing volunteer projects. We will organize at least two seasonal work days each year (spring and fall) to weed, prune, replenish mulch, and replace any plants that do not survive the winter. The White Pine Parks Department has provided written approval for the project and has indicated that, as long as volunteers continue basic upkeep, they will assist with larger tasks such as hauling debris and checking the irrigation system.

Personally, I live three blocks from the park and already walk my dog there daily, so I am prepared to keep an eye on plant health, trash, and vandalism and to coordinate quick fixes with the volunteer group. Over time, we hope this garden will demonstrate to residents that native, pollinator-friendly plantings are not only beautiful but also practical in our climate, and that even a modest, community-driven project can create a meaningful outdoor learning space for children and adults alike.
